posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
Last night a small group got together and tasted a few assorted 2013 Burgundies to get a handle on the vintage. I personally felt this would be an eye opener for some. The vintage is MUCH better than has been hyped especially in red. We single blinded a few wines just for fun but many knew the wines. It was interesting however the focus that you get from blinding. It really takes the label out of the equation. I have to thank Eric Gerber for the prod to get this together. He was the inspiration and donated unselfishly to make this happen. Thanks Eric!

2013 Pierre-Yves Colin Morey(PYCM) St Aubin Les Chatenieres

This wine was so good. A beautiful nose. Golden fruit. Sexy nose. Wonderful balance of fruit/acid. This slayed everyone at the table. Many noted that this really drank so much better than they imagined. First rate St Aubin.

2013 Domaine Chavy Puligny Montrachet

This wine was a little less stylish and polished. It was precise and showed a nice amount of fruit. The acid was a little in front on this one. We didn’t decant and it needed it. A very nice wine nonetheless.

Blinded - 2 Chassagne Premier Crus

Wine 1- This wine was SO good. A nose of golden fruit. Sexy. Stylish. To me this was PYCM. The wines are stylized some but I like the style! The mid palate was full here and the finish incredibly long. One of my friends asked if we had started on the Grand crus already. A person whose palate is second to none. Nope. 2013 PYCM Chassagne Montrachet Les Caillerets An oh my goodness wine.

Wine 2- This was precise and crystalline. Neutral in presentation. Very mineral. The fruit here is less golden. Less stylish use of oaking too. Here there was a little acid out front but the wine was terrific. 2013 Colin-Deleger Chassagne Montrachet en Remilly (Bruno Colin)

Blinded - 2 Grand Crus

Wine 1- This wine was so feminine. Mineral. Gold fruit but very subtle wine. More about balance than power. Fabulous length. Young and will be incredible. 2013 Paul Pernot Bienvenue Batard Montrachet.

Wine 2- (N.B. this was decanted for a while) Unctuous in the glass and with a precision that you don’t expect given that huge palate. Huge staining mid palate with whiter fruit. Stone fruit type. The finish is extraordinarily long. Incredible. Unevolved too but you can tell this should turn into something special if no premox. 2013 Colin- Deleger Chevalier Montrachet (Philippe Colin)


2013 Domaine Fourrier Gevrey Chambertin VV

Very tight on opening. Light color. Bright fruit and lots of it. Redder than you would expect for Gevrey. The finish opens a little with time. I liked this more than most. The acid was slightly in front on this one.

2013 Henri Jouan Gevrey Chambertin aux Echezeaux

Absolutely stunning nose. Darker fruit with some element of earthiness. A note of stem inclusion to me. Like older Dujac wines when Jacques was making them in many ways. The palate is darker and complex even at this early date. Great ripeness and volume here. For this appellation, this was awe-inspiring.

Blinded- Two Chambolles

Wine 1- Fruit jumps out of this glass. Sexy. Red. The palate is full and goes on for quite some time. Stylishly done with some complexity added from the elevage but not distracting. Absolutely fabulous. 2013 Francois Bertheau Chambolle Musigny Charmes

Wine 2- Old school here. Some thought four square but I did not. Darker fruit. Very brooding in the glass but this unwinds. At a short tasting like this, you don’t get to really see what this wine is giving. There is power and grace here. The core of red fruit comes out on the end. I knew this one the moment it was presented. Terrific! 2013 Digioia- Royer Chambolle Musigny Gruenchers

Blinded - Two Chambolles

Wine 1- Ethereal nose. Red fruit. Nothing out of place. About balance and graceful presentation. The finish is Grand Cru intensity. Amazing. My friend with the great palate thought this was worth whatever money you have to pay for this. 2013 Francois Bertheau Chambolle Musigny Les Amoureuses

Wine 2- The nose shows some coffee notes from the elevage. Darker fruit here. Elegant presentation and quite stylish. The finish is great. 2013 Mugneret- Gibourg Chambolle Musigny Les Feusselots

Blinded - Two Grand Crus

Wine 1- This wine is a little closed in. The nose shows hints of coffee and very stylized darker fruits. The finish is intense and a little brooding in a way. Elegant wine however. 2013 Mugneret-Gibourg Clos Vougeot (Decanted by me. Next time I wouldn’t decant if I had it young. Needs time)

Wine 2 - This one is full on expressive. A huge nose of darker fruits and savory elements. All kinds of crazy savory notes on the palate too. Powerful red/black fruit that persists forever. This one is so full and intense. Wow. 2013 Domaine Jean-Yves Bizot Echezeaux (new to me)

Blinded - Two Grand Crus

Wine 1- Feminine in many ways but with power on the finish. This has a gorgeous nose of darker fruit. Firm sense of minerality. Long finish. I loved it. 2013 Domaine Bart Chambertin Clos de Beze

Wine 2- Full on expressive with darker fruits. So complex on the nose and in the mouth. Incredible sense of balance here. Extraordinary finish. Dujac!
A true WOW wine. Wished I had some. 2013 Dujac Clos St Denis

Bonus

2013 Guillard Gevrey Chambertin Les Corbeaux
After all those terrific wines before this was not out of place. Darker fruit. Great balance here. That is the calling card. It is always difficult to taste a premier cru after those big boys but I really liked this. Thanks John!

Thanks to all the guys who made it to the tasting. We had some cheese from Saquachie Creamery in Tennessee. If you ever get a chance to try the Nikajack (a Reblochon washed rind type), you should give it a go. It is raw milk. Stinky and delicious! Some smoked duck from Sonoma.

It was a fun evening.

posts.name: Carlos Delpin
posts.cooked: Thanks for sharing. Good insights.

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked: Thanks Carlos. I finally got the notes in a readable form.
Great tasting, Don. I can’t say that I’ve had a 2013 that I didn’t like. I love the fruit profile in this vintage, for Reds in particular.

thanks.
had only really good experience with 2013…and i buy again and again…and after your notes also Feusselottes again.

posts.name: Brad England
posts.cooked: Nice notes Don, thanks. Oddly I passed on the 2013 Dujac CSD - sounds like a mistake as I love their CSD across all vintages. I have a few of the PYCM Caillerets. Am I reading your note right to say go ahead and drink these early (as in start now)?

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
Brad. That Dujac was a show stopper.

This tasting was just for getting a snapshot. All the wines need time really. We just popped a few in the name of science.

Very good balance in this group of 2013s. Very few with the acid overwhelming the fruit which was the hype I had heard from a few folks. That was what we wanted to check out.

posts.name: CHIU LIN
posts.cooked:
Thanks for the notes, I have bought a few on the list but yet to open any.

Sadly I passed up on Jouan Aux Echezeaux as I already bought a case of '12 vintage, love that wine, can rival Fourrier’s version.

posts.name: Robert Pavlovich
posts.cooked: Agreed on 2013 reds Don. There’s maybe just a little more freshness than some would like, but if you like your acid medium plus and beyond, it’s really nice.

posts.name: Alan Rath
posts.cooked:
dcornutt:

2013 Domaine Fourrier Gevrey Chambertin VV
Very tight on opening. Light color. Bright fruit and lots of it. Redder than you would expect for Gevrey. The finish opens a little with time. I liked this more than most. The acid was slightly in front on this one.

Thanks for all the very useful notes. Happy to hear about the PYCM Caillerets. The Fourrier sounds unusually tame, given its normal lush, bigger style.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Really nice notes, which very much align with my own perceptions. Spot on about the PYCM Caillerets. I often think that wine is as good as any he makes, often surpassing the higher appellation negoce wines to my palate. And good to be reminded about how successful Bart and Jouan so often are. I remember tasting the 13 Dujac CSD with Jeremy and being similarly impressed by its aromatic amplitude.

Did you have a sense of the optimal time to start approaching these wines in earnest?

posts.name: Don Cornutt
posts.cooked:
Thanks for all the kind words.

re: approaching these wines.

They don’t seem to be loaded with tannin so I would think the wait won’t be incredibly long.
The only wild card here is the vibrant acid. Not 1996 vibrant but higher than most of the recent vintages to me by appearance. I am going to give them age 8 to 10 for a return.
Thanks for the pycm check in, just bought a couple of the St… Aubins Thursday, excited to try them…

posts.name: Michel Linden
posts.cooked:
this is encouraging as I bought some wines year in year out so I continue to get the offers
much appreciated

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Thanks Don. I really like '13 in both colours.

Best Regards
Jeremy

posts.name: D@v1dZ
posts.cooked: While I’ve had some good 2013s, I think 2013 is not good at lower levels, and especially so with less ripe vineyards. Certainly it’s not as good as 2008, despite the passing resemblance with the acids - 2008 had more stuffing, even though the acids were also high. I have been sticking to vineyards with the riper ESE exposures with pretty good results; a decent Marsannay Champs Solomon tonight, for example, a decent Gevrey Aux Echezeaux a few weeks back. I suspect based on past experience that this will also be a vintage that favors downslope vineyards.

posts.name: Martin Steinley
posts.cooked:
Don, this is a great snapshot of a diverse bunch of good wines. Thank you for taking the time and making the effort to post the good notes.

Separately, I don’t understand how one can possibly make an accurate pronouncement that the 2013 Burgundy vintage is better or worse than this vintage or that at this stage; even leaving aside the question of “for what?” There is, however, the benefit of buying opportunities being created when enough get it wrong on the side of an inferior vintage.

posts.name: D@v1dZ
posts.cooked:
Martin Steinley:

Don, this is a great snapshot of a diverse bunch of good wines. Thank you for taking the time and making the effort to post the good notes.

Separately, I don’t understand how one can possibly make an accurate pronouncement that the 2013 Burgundy vintage is better or worse than this vintage or that at this stage; even leaving aside the question of “for what?” There is, however, the benefit of buying opportunities being created when enough get it wrong on the side of an inferior vintage.

You can’t put yourself in your own shoes drinking the 2008s five years ago? Especially with the hindsight of seeing how they are evolving now? I find that surprising.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: No two vintages are ever exactly alike. In fact as they evolve the differences become more pronounced. Of course YMMV, and I have little doubt it does.

Don, thank you for all the leg work and your extreme generosity. That spread of food was perfect. What am immensely enjoyable night!

I was a fan of the 2013s coming in to this tasting but even more so after last night. The whites are very good (though not exceptional) and the reds are great mid-term, middle weight gems.

On specifics wines - I find the PYCM wines so compelling in their minerality and precision but they always need time for me to get past the suplhur. On the plus side, they likely will be around past 5 years!

The Chavy Puligny jumped out of the glass and leaves a strong palate impression. A great wine to pair with richer fare.

The Chevalier was my white of the night. More of a whisper and so well balanced. One taster called out “rainwater”. I agree. Went back for seconds.

The Jouan - Fourrier side by side was interesting. The Fourrier seemed a bit over extracted compared to the Jouan. Very different styles, of course, but I don’t usually have that experience of Fourrier despite the lushness. Will let my '13 Fourriers rest a bit.

The Chambolles were just great. Bertheau was tops for me. The Charmes was fantastic, the Amoureuses spectacular! Another one I went back for seconds on. I think the Mugneret-Gibourg needs at least another year to integrate. Had the same experience a few weeks ago with another bottle (the oak sticks out just a bit). The Digioia-Royer needed time to unfurl and suffered some in comparison to the other more graceful wines on the table. Would like to try that one again.

Wine of the night for me was the Dujac Clos St Denis. Dayuuumm it was good. Can’t win to see what 15-20 years brings. So well balanced and elegant. Bizot was new to me as well and a very nice red fruited example of Echezeaux. The Clos Vougeout was still bound up tight but you can tell there is so much to come with that wine. I enjoyed the Bart Beze but admittedly was hard for me to get a good read on what the future looks like.

I will close with the thought that as enjoyable as the wines were, the people around the table made the night. Thanks, guys, for the good natured fun, laughs and camaraderie. Until next time,
